Event: Roger offers to promote Ken to partner in exchange for Ken getting his father-in-law to sign on as an account
Explanation: Roger seeks personal/business gain via quid pro quo that pressures Ken and exploits his family tie.

Event: Ken turns down Roger’s offer because he does not want to get involved with office politics
Explanation: Ken sacrifices quick advancement to preserve integrity and stability while modestly limiting others’ manipulative games.

Event: Don goes to bat for Ken to keep his job when he was fired
Explanation: Don expends capital to protect Ken’s employment, benefitting Ken greatly and costing Don little.

Event: Don takes time to empathize with Ken and try to help him when Ken is despairing over being fired
Explanation: Don offers emotional support and practical help that improves Ken’s well-being with minimal downside to himself.

Event: Ken gleefully makes Pete’s life miserable after taking a position at Dow Chemical
Explanation: Ken uses his new power to punish Pete in ways that gratify him but substantially hurt Pete professionally.

Event: Ken gleefully makes Roger’s life miserable after taking a position at Dow Chemical
Explanation: Ken retaliates against Roger through his control over Dow, enjoying the payback while damaging Roger’s interests.

Event: Ken is quite friendly with Don after joining Dow because Don previously supported him
Explanation: Ken rewards Don’s past kindness with favorable treatment, strengthening a mutually beneficial relationship.

Event: The Chevy executives involve Ken in a car crash
Explanation: The execs’ negligence or recklessness harms Ken physically without clear benefit to him, while they likely maintain their own standing.

Event: The Chevy executives shoot Ken on a hunting trip
Explanation: The hunting “accident” severely injures Ken while the shooters largely avoid consequences, preserving their position.

Event: Ken gives the Chevy account to Pete because of the trauma
Explanation: Kenn’s withdrawal relieves his own stress somewhat but costs him prestige and opportunity while benefiting Pete’s career.

Event: Ken calls it quits on the account and hands it to Pete after accidents
Explanation: Ken steps away from a major account (career loss) to escape further harm, passing a lucrative opportunity to Pete.

Event: Ken in early series takes advantage of office politics for his own gain
Explanation: He leverages internal politics to advance himself at the expense of peers’ standing.

Event: Ken starts honestly and vocally appreciating Peggy's creative work
Explanation: Ken’s sincere praise supports Peggy’s development and reputation while also aligning him with quality work.

Event: Ken settles down and gets married, becoming faithful to his wife
Explanation: Marriage and fidelity give him stability and happiness and benefit his spouse emotionally.

Event: Ken is every bit the womanizer in early seasons
Explanation: He pursues casual flings for his own gratification with limited or unclear benefit to the women involved.

Event: Ken treats Peggy with respect when hitting on her
Explanation: While interested romantically, he behaves respectfully, mildly benefiting himself and significantly benefiting Peggy versus the norm.

Event: Ken tries (unsuccessfully) to court Jane
Explanation: He attempts a respectful courtship; failure leaves him roughly neutral and offers Jane agency and attention without clear harm.

Event: Ken gets engaged and then remains faithful to his wife
Explanation: Commitment and fidelity deepen mutual trust and long-term wellbeing for both partners.

Event: Ken attempts to cover up his missing eye with an eyepatch to look more distinctive
Explanation: The eyepatch slightly helps his self-presentation and others’ ability to recognize/relate to him.

Event: Ken loses an eye to a hunting accident
Explanation: The accident gravely harms Ken while others emerge essentially unaffected.

Event: Ken respects Peggy and treats her relatively equally long before peers do
Explanation: His early egalitarian treatment aids Peggy’s confidence and career while also aligning him with a rising talent.

Event: Ken and Peggy develop into a very effective team when going after clients
Explanation: Their collaboration boosts their reputations and brings in business, benefitting themselves and the firm.

Event: Ken writes and publishes over 20 SFF stories under a pseudonym
Explanation: He gains creative fulfillment and prestige while providing entertainment and value to readers.

Event: Roger gives Ken a tongue-lashing for dividing his focus on writing
Explanation: Roger asserts authority and vents jealousy, undermining Ken’s confidence more than it helps him refocus.

Event: Ken fiddles around with getting some of his writings published
Explanation: Pursuing publication benefits his self-expression and status and, aside from jealousy, doesn’t materially harm others.

Event: Ken succeeds in getting his writings published, provoking colleagues' jealousy
Explanation: His success elevates him but indirectly harms jealous peers’ egos and standing.

Event: Ken quits his position at McCann when approached by the Partners to work for SCDP
Explanation: The move advances his interests with SCDP while damaging McCann by losing him unexpectedly.

Event: McCann makes it their first priority to fire Ken after buying SC&P, in retaliation for his earlier quitting
Explanation: They satisfy their desire for payback and consolidation while seriously harming Ken’s job security.

Event: Roger does not try to fight for Ken’s job when McCann fires him
Explanation: Roger avoids political or personal cost, abandoning Ken to significant loss.

Event: Senior staff favor Ken over Pete in early seasons
Explanation: They back Ken’s reliability at the expense of Pete’s advancement.

Event: Ken refuses to get involved in office politics, limiting his advancement
Explanation: He trades rapid promotion for integrity and stability, balancing modest self-costs with broader organizational sanity.

Event: Partners do not initially deem Ken essential enough to bring to the newly formed SCDP
Explanation: They protect their core group, sidelining Ken and hurting his short-term prospects.

Event: Partners eventually hire Ken when the company is on more stable financial footing
Explanation: Bringing him back improves his career and strengthens the agency with a reliable account man.

Event: Ken fat-shames Peggy in season one
Explanation: He asserts social dominance or humor at Peggy’s expense, harming her self-image without real gain for himself.

Event: Ken’s prejudice against the Irish leads to his firing after the McCann-Erickson takeover
Explanation: His bigotry harms others and directly causes his own serious job loss.

Event: Ken continues making prejudiced remarks even after they contributed to his firing
Explanation: He persists in behavior that brings him no benefit and risks further reputational damage while offending others.

Event: Ken’s stress from managing virtually every account damages his temper
Explanation: Overwork harms his mental state and behavior without bringing commensurate long-term benefit.

Event: Ken snaps at Joan under extreme duress
Explanation: He vents frustration on Joan, slightly relieving stress while clearly upsetting her and straining relations.

Event: Ken later apologizes to Joan for his irritability
Explanation: He repairs the relationship, restoring trust and improving the work environment for both.

Event: Ken is genuinely pleased to see Don and reminisces about the carousel in Central Park reminding him of Don
Explanation: He affirms their bond and Don’s legacy, strengthening goodwill with little cost.

Event: Roger and McCann fire Ken in Season 7’s "Severance"
Explanation: They remove a perceived problem and consolidate power while inflicting major career harm on Ken.

Event: Ken takes over his father-in-law's position at Dow Chemicals after being fired
Explanation: He secures a powerful new role and provides continuity and value to Dow.

Event: Ken calmly tells Roger and Pete that they will now need to please him
Explanation: He asserts dominance and enjoys their discomfort, benefiting himself while reducing their autonomy.

Event: Ken strings Roger and Pete along for a few months over Dow business
Explanation: He exacts drawn-out revenge, gaining satisfaction and leverage while wasting their time and harming their plans.

Event: Ken gleefully fires SC&P as Dow’s agency, blocking their California move
Explanation: He gains emotional satisfaction and tactical advantage at Dow while severely damaging SC&P’s strategic goal.
